  he historiographic formula   lavia rthodoa , together with its counterpart   lavia omana   (also
referred to as   lavia atholica ), was introduced by icchio ] the terms reflect the 'division of
historical lavdom into two main areas belonging to the jurisdiction of the astern rthodo hurches
( lavia rthodoa ) and to that of the oman hurch ( lavia omana )'  ibid.  ]. ollowing icchio's
methodology,  approach the institutionalised partition of entral and astern urope between ome
and onstantinople as a   sui generis   linguistic phenomenon atin was to function as the   lingua sacra   in
 lavia omana , while in   lavia rthodoa   this role was played by ld hurch lavonic.  further
argue that, along with   lavia omana   and   lavia rthodoa,   another set of terms, reflecting the
confessional identity of 'other' religious communities (be it hristian, ewish, or uslim) should be
taken into consideration, with special emphasis on their respective   linguae sacrae  hence my argument
for   lavia vangelica ,   lavia udaica   and   lavia slamica   adalanova   ]. he
linguistic differentiation between   lavia omana   and   lavia rthodoa   (i.e. atin versus ld hurch
lavonic) had a major impact upon future cleavage between the respective cultural traditions 'within
each of these two main areas of civilisation, the self-identification of the lavs with certain cultural and
linguistic systems was directly affected by the ideological and linguistic models that the ecclesiastical
organisations introduced into their spiritual patrimony' icchio  ] see also icchio and
oldblatt  -]. he fact that the 'spiritual patrimony' of   lavia rthodoa   was anchored by
ld hurch lavonic eplains why    noch   was not attested in apocryphal heritage of   lavia omana
 and remained a specific product of   lavia rthodoa   eclusively.   owever, folklore evidence
indicates that 'the noch pos' penetrated the domain of vernacular oral tradition of hristian
communities in zones of mied or overlapping influence between   lavia rthodoa   and   lavia
omana , such as the reco-atholics (also known as atholics of yzantine rite) in the arpathian
region (i.e. uthenians, or ysyns). olodymyr natiuk, for instance, published two fragments of folk
